records.ety: [F., fr. L. fabula, fr. fari to speak, say. See Ban, and cf. Fabulous, Fame.]

defs: A Feigned story or tale, intended to instruct or amuse; a fictitious narration intended to enforce some useful truth or precept; an apologue. See the Note under Apologue.
quotes: Jotham's fable of the trees is the oldest extant.
notes: A fable may have talking animals anthropomorphically cast as humans representing different character types, sometimes illustrating some moral principle; as, Aesop's Fables.

defs: The plot, story, or connected series of events, forming the subject of an epic or dramatic poem.
quotes: The moral is the first business of the poet; this being formed, he contrives such a design or fable as may be most suitable to the moral.

defs: Any story told to excite wonder; common talk; the theme of talk.
quotes: We grew The fable of the city where we dwelt.
It would look like a fable to report that this gentleman gives away a great fortune by secret methods.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Fabled (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Fabling (?).]

defs: To compose fables; hence, to write or speak fiction ; to write or utter what is not true.
quotes: Vain now the tales which fabling poets tell.

defs: To feign; to invent; to devise, and speak of, as true or real; to tell of falsely.
